skill.name: hive-mind-advanced
skill.description: Guides advanced Claude Flow hive-mind coordination for multi-agent planning, execution, consensus, memory, and session management.
skill.summary: Coordinate Claude Flow hive-mind agents with queen roles, consensus workflows, memory, and sessions.
skill.author: Claude Flow Team

security_audit.summary: Static external-command findings are true positives because the skill documents many npx claude-flow commands, but they are instructional examples for the advertised tool rather than hidden execution logic. The weak-cryptography findings are false positives on prose, metadata, and comments, and the hardcoded URLs are GitHub documentation links. No prompt injection attempt or malicious data exfiltration intent was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: External command examples require confirmation before execution
medium_findings.description: The skill contains many documented shell commands that invoke npx claude-flow for initialization, spawning, status checks, memory operations, session management, export, and import. This matches the skill purpose, but agents should not run these commands automatically because npx can execute external packages and the commands can modify local project or Claude Flow state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present in Markdown code blocks and align with the skill purpose. Confidence is high for the execution risk, but there is no evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded GitHub documentation links
low_findings.description: The skill includes four static GitHub documentation links in the references section. These links are user-visible documentation references and do not show secret transmission, tracking endpoints, or hidden network calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL targets are visible GitHub documentation paths. No code in the skill sends data to those URLs.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography detections are false positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography hits occur on metadata, role labels, comments, and prose such as Hive Mind wording, architect labels, and result descriptions. I found no cryptographic API, hashing implementation, or weak algorithm use at those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual line review shows ordinary documentation text at each flagged location. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar weak crypto usage.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown shell commands using npx
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly shows npx claude-flow shell commands. This is expected documentation for Claude Flow workflows, but marketplace users should treat execution as an explicit action requiring review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly visible in command examples. The risk is contextual because the commands support the stated workflow rather than hidden behavior.

content.value_statement: Complex Claude Flow work can require many specialized agents, shared memory, and clear decision rules. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured guidance for hive-mind orchestration, consensus, sessions, and monitoring.
actual_capabilities: Explains queen-led coordination patterns for strategic, tactical, and adaptive hive-mind workflows.
Documents Claude Flow commands for hive-mind initialization, swarm spawning, status checks, metrics, and memory review.
Describes majority, weighted, and Byzantine consensus models for multi-agent decisions.
Covers collective memory concepts, including storage types, search, association, cache behavior, and persistence.
Provides session management guidance for pause, resume, stop, checkpoint, export, and import workflows.
Shows integration patterns for Claude Code, SPARC workflows, GitHub review tasks, and full-stack development.
limitations: It is documentation and workflow guidance, not an executable hive-mind engine by itself.
It assumes Claude Flow and its hive-mind commands are installed and available in the user environment.
Command examples may change if Claude Flow changes its CLI behavior or options.
It does not verify command safety, dependency integrity, or repository permissions before execution.

use_cases.title: Plan Multi-Agent Development Work
use_cases.description: Use the skill to define queen roles, worker roles, consensus rules, and monitoring steps for a complex implementation plan.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Repository Review
use_cases.description: Apply hive-mind patterns to split review work across security, performance, test coverage, and documentation perspectives.

use_cases.title: Manage Long-Running Agent Sessions
use_cases.description: Use the session, checkpoint, memory, and export guidance to keep multi-agent work resumable and observable.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Hive Mind
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the hive-mind-advanced skill to plan a basic Claude Flow hive-mind session for this objective: [objective]. Include the queen type, worker roles, consensus mode, and status checks.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Agent Roles
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the hive-mind-advanced skill to map this project into queen and worker responsibilities: [project]. Explain which agents should handle research, coding, testing, review, and documentation.

prompt_templates.title: Design Consensus and Memory
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the hive-mind-advanced skill to design consensus rules and collective memory usage for this multi-agent task: [task]. Include decision thresholds, stored knowledge types, and retrieval needs.

prompt_templates.title: Operate a Full Session
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the hive-mind-advanced skill to create an operating plan for a long-running hive-mind session: [objective]. Include initialization, spawn strategy, monitoring, checkpoints, failure handling, and export steps.

output_examples.input: Plan a hive mind to build an authentication service.
output_examples.output: A tactical queen coordinates backend, database, security, testing, and documentation workers. Weighted consensus is used for implementation choices, while checkpoints track route completion, token design, test coverage, and release readiness.

output_examples.input: Review a pull request with multiple risk areas.
output_examples.output: The workflow assigns reviewers for code quality, security, performance, tests, and documentation. The queen gathers findings, requests consensus on blocking issues, and stores reusable review patterns in collective memory.
The session plan checks active sessions, resumes the target session, reviews memory and metrics, validates remaining tasks, and creates a new checkpoint after work restarts.

best_practices: Review every generated shell command before execution, especially npx commands and commands that modify project state.
Choose the queen type and consensus mode based on task risk, complexity, and need for decisive control.
Use collective memory for durable decisions, assumptions, results, and lessons that future agents should reuse.
anti_patterns: Do not let agents execute hive-mind commands without user confirmation and a clear working directory.
Do not use complex consensus modes for simple one-step tasks that do not need multiple agents.
Do not store secrets, credentials, or private customer data in shared collective memory.

faq.question: Does this skill run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents command patterns and workflows. Users or agents must explicitly decide whether to run any command.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What is a queen agent?
faq.answer: A queen agent coordinates strategy, assigns work, and guides consensus across specialized worker agents.

faq.question: When should I use Byzantine consensus?
faq.answer: Use it for higher-risk decisions where stronger agreement matters more than fast progress.

faq.question: Does it include persistent memory guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains memory types, search, associations, cache behavior, persistence, and session-related storage.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill contains many external command examples. They appear legitimate, but command execution still requires review and user approval.
